titleStmt.title: Letter from C. O'Connell Fitzsimon to Henry Arthur Wynne, 4 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: C. O'Connell Fitzsimon

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter was written by Christopher O'Connell Fitzsimon (1860 - 1947), a solicitor from Queen's County. The letter was addressed to Sir Henry Arthur Wynne (1867 - 1945). Wynne was appointed to the vacant post of chief crown solicitor for Ireland following the death of Sir Malachy Kelly (1851-1916).This letter concerns the application of William Phelan, of Ballinakill Queen's County, for an extension of his six-day early closing licence under the Licensing Act, 1902.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
ABBEYLEIX. 4th July, 1916. Dear Sir, On the 26th June last an application was was made on behalf
of Wm. Phelan of Ballinakill, Queen's County, the Holder of
a sixâday early closing licence for an ordinary sixâday
licence, and said application was granted by the Justices at
Abbeyleix Quarter Sessions under the impression that Section
2 of the Licensing Act, 1902, authorised the granting of
such a certificate. Since the Order was granted, the cases decided on the
Act have been looked into, and the applicant, being satisfied
that there was no jurisdiction to make the order in his
favour, instructs me to inform you that he does not propose
to take any advantage of the order or apply to the Inland
Revenue for any extension of his licence, and will, if it is
considered necessary, consent to the said order being
quashed. Yours faithfully, C OConnell Fitzsimon The Chief Crown Solicitor for Ireland, The Castle, Dublin.
